step.title: Step 60
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 9 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 87, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves character right by 3 cols', 'DOWN': 'Moves character down by 3 rows', 'LEFT': 'Moves character left by 3 cols', 'UP': 'Moves character up by 3 rows', 'SPACE': 'Rotates/transforms character'}
- Object taxonomy: {'white_shape': 'Player character (P-shape)', 'red_squares': '3x3 red blocks', 'green_frame': 'Green frame template (P-shape)', 'gray_blocks': 'Gray blocks from interactions'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'charcoal bar at row 0'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - game over reached without completing level
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Game over at step 76, score 0. Did not complete level.'}
Current level:
- World model: Game over state. Character is white P-shape. Green frame is also P-shape. Red blocks are 3x3 squares. Gray blocks appear from interactions.
- Goal model: Fit character into green frame by rotating and moving
- Recent findings: ['Character shape changes with rotation (SPACE)', 'Character moves in 3-cell increments (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT)']
- Open questions: ['What exact configuration completes the level?']
- Plan: Game over - no more actions possible
- Strategy log: ['Cycling through orientations and moving character did not complete level - game over reached']
- Failed probes: ['Multiple SPACE rotations without completing level', 'Multiple UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves without completing level']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 86 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 3.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 76 actions returned the board to a state already seen 76 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 76 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good progress! Looking at the current image:
- The white P-shape character has moved to the right and is now overlapping with the green P-frame
- The red blocks are still visible below the white character
- The green frame is partially covered by the white character
The white character is now at approximately the same horizontal position as the green frame. I need to move it DOWN to align vertically with the green frame.
From the previous output:
- White: pixels=135, boundary corners: [[11, 14], [11, 28], [19, 28], [19, 26]]
- Green: pixels=144, boundary corners: [[29, 41], [29, 49], [49, 49], [49, 41]]
After 9 RIGHT moves, the white character is at col 14-28 (approximately). The green frame is at col 41-49. So I still need to move RIGHT more to align horizontally.
Wait, looking at the current image, the white character is now at the top right, and the green frame is at the bottom right. The white character is above the green frame, not overlapping it.
Let me recalculate:
- White character: rows 11-19, cols 14-28 (approximately)
- Green frame: rows 29-49, cols 41-49 (approximately)
To move from row 11 to row 29: (29-11)/3 = 6 DOWN moves
To move from col 14 to col 41: (41-14)/3 = 9 RIGHT moves (already done)
Wait, but the white character is at col 14-28, and the green frame is at col 41-49. So I still need to move RIGHT more.
Actually, looking at the current image more carefully:
- The white character is at the top right
- The green frame is at the bottom right
- The white character is above the green frame, not overlapping it
So I need to move DOWN to align vertically with the green frame. Let me try 6 DOWN moves.
But wait, I also need to consider the orientation. The white character is an upright P, and the green frame appears to be a P-shape that's oriented differently. Looking at the green frame, it seems like it's a P-shape that's rotated 180 degrees (upside down P) or maybe it's a mirrored P.
Let me first move the character to the green frame's position, then rotate if needed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"White: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"Green: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")

# Move DOWN to align vertically with green frame
# White at row ~11-19, green at row ~29-49
# Need (29-11)/3 = 6 DOWN moves
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves character right by 3 cols", "DOWN": "Moves character down by 3 rows", "LEFT": "Moves character left by 3 cols", "UP": "Moves character up by 3 rows", "SPACE": "Rotates/transforms character"}, "object_taxonomy": {"white_shape": "Player character (P-shape)", "red_squares": "3x3 red blocks", "green_frame": "Green frame template (P-shape)", "gray_blocks": "Gray blocks from interactions"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "charcoal bar at row 0"}, "win_pattern": "Fit white P-shape into green P-frame by moving and rotating", "level_log": {"level_1": "Moved character RIGHT 10 times. Now need to move DOWN to green frame."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "White P-shape at top-right, green P-frame at bottom-right. Need to move DOWN to align.", "goal_model": "Move character to green frame position and rotate to match orientation", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT moves work, character is now horizontally aligned"], "open_questions": ["What rotation matches green frame?"], "current_plan": "Move DOWN 6 times to reach green frame vertically", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
